4.             OTHER INFORMATION

(a)           Indemnity and other dealing arrangements

Details of any indemnity or option arrangement, or any agreement or understanding, formal or informal, relating to relevant securities which may be an inducement to deal or refrain from dealing entered into by the person making the disclosure and any party to the offer or any person acting in concert with a party to the offer.

Irrevocable commitments and letters of intent should not be included. If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

(b)           Agreements, arrangements, or understandings relating to options or derivatives

Full details of any agreement, arrangement or understanding between the person disclosing and any other person relating to the voting rights of any relevant securities under any option referred to on this form or relating to the voting rights or future acquisition or disposal of any relevant securities to which any derivative referred to on this form is referenced. If none, this should be stated.

NOTES ON FORM 8.3

1.             See the definition of "connected fund manager" in Rule 2.2 of Part A of the Rules.

2.             See the definition of "interest in a relevant security" in Rule 2.5 of Part A of the Rules and see Rule 8.6(a) and (b) of Part B of the Rules.

3.             See the definition of "relevant securities" in Rule 2.1 of Part A of the Rules.

4.             See the definition of "dealing" in Rule 2.1 of Part A of the Rules.

5.             If the economic exposure to changes in the price of securities is limited, for example, by virtue of a stop loss arrangement relating to a spread bet, full details must be given.

6.             See Rule 2.5(d) of Part A of the Rules.

7.             If details included in a disclosure under Rule 8 are incorrect, they should be corrected as soon as practicable in a subsequent disclosure. Such disclosure should state clearly that it corrects details disclosed previously, identify the disclosure or disclosures being corrected, and provide sufficient detail for the reader to understand the nature of the corrections. In the case of any doubt, the Panel should be consulted.

For full details of disclosure requirements, see Rule 8 of the Rules. If in doubt, consult the Panel.

References in these notes to "the Rules" are to the Irish Takeover Panel Act, 1997, Takeover Rules, 2022.

data.title: Hong Kong Stocks Slip Below 24,000 Points on Deflation Concerns amid Trade Tensions; SMIC Drops 5%, Wise Ally International Soars 109%
data.description:
Hong Kong stocks fell below 24,000 points on Monday as consumer prices again showed contraction in February, albeit lower than polled by analysts, raising fears of deflation among the investors on top of the growing trade and geopolitical tensions.

The Hang Seng Index fell 1.85%, or 447.81 points, to close at 23,783.49. The Hang Seng China Enterprises Index dropped 2.12%, or 189.02 points, to end at 8,725.01.

China's consumer price index contracted 0.7% year over year in February, the first time the indicator returned to negative territory since January 2024. Analysts polled by Reuters estimated CPI to have contracted 0.5%.

The producer price index continued contracting, this time by 2.2% in February, easing from a 2.3% slide in January and lower than a 2.1% decline forecast by analysts polled by the news agency. The indicator has been falling since September 2022.

On the geopolitical front, China slapped extra tariffs against selected Canadian imports such as rapeseed oil, oil cakes and peas, aquatic products, and pork, effective March 20.

The US Trade Representative will also conduct a probe on China-manufactured "legacy" chip models used for consumer electronics. The hearing could mean more tariffs against China. Semiconductor Manufacturing International, or SMIC , fell nearly 5% on Monday.

In corporate news, Chifeng Jilong Gold Mining , witnessed a tepid stock market debut as its shares opened flat on their maiden trading in Hong Kong Monday morning. It closed unchanged at HK$13.72.

Wise Ally International's shares soared nearly 109% as the company expects an attributable profit of between HK$60 million and HK$80 million for the year 2024, up from HK$1.2 million a year prior.

Tongguan Gold Group unit Grand Gallant Investments agreed to purchase Wise Trend Investment from Jumbo China for up to HK$400 million, subject to shareholder approval. Shares of the company jumped nearly 27% on Monday's close.

data.title: India's Sterlite Technologies drops as CFO resigns
data.description:
** Shares of Sterlite Technologies fall 3.8% to 82.5 rupees

** Network solutions provider's CFO Tushar Shroff resigns

** Co is in process of appointing new CFO

** More than 2 mln shares traded, 1.5x the 30-day avg

** Stock trading below 50-, 100-, 200- day moving avgs

** Stock has shed 25% this year, on top of a ~20% decline in 2024

data.title: Gold Futures Flat as Market Awaits U.S. Economic Data — Market Talk
data.description:
0919 GMT - Gold futures are broadly flat in relatively muted trading. Futures are flat on $2,912.10 a troy ounce. The precious metal sits up 0.4% on week on lingering safe-haven demand and a weaker U.S. dollar. That said, gold is trading in a tight range as markets appear to be pricing out the inflationary effect of president Trump's tariff policies given constant reversals, Sucden Financial's Daria Efanova says in a note. Market attention is fixed on Wednesday's U.S. Consumer Price Index data, and Producer Price Index data set for release on Thursday. The economic data could provide more clues as to the Federal Reserve's pathway to monetary policy easing. Lower interest rates typically raise the appeal of non-interest bearing bullion. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0914 GMT - 2025 could be a key year for humanoid robots launches, Daiwa analysts say in a research note. Some Chinese robot makers, like Unitree and AgiBot, have already reduced unit costs to around 100,000 yuan, with the potential for further reductions of more than 50% upon mass-production, they say. Daiwa expects more than one million humanoid robot units to be produced by 2030, while further AI breakthroughs could enhance robots' responsiveness and accelerate their adoption. They anticipate that humanoid robots will be initially used in manufacturing and logistics before expanding into the construction and mining industries. Several leading global tech companies, including Huawei and Meta, have already announced humanoid robot products. Daiwa expects these development to boost sentiment, establish an entirely new supply chain, and particularly benefit component suppliers. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0904 GMT - Bitcoin falls as cryptocurrencies come under pressure from U.S. regulatory uncertainty and widespread risk aversion, Zaye Capital Markets analyst Naeem Aslam says in a note. A crypto summit at the White House on Friday did not provide any details on President Trump's plans for regulation of the sector. Markets are also grappling with concerns over global trade tensions, deflationary pressures in China and the prospect of a weakening U.S. economy, Aslam says. Bitcoin falls 5.5% to $81,493 after hitting a one-week low of $80,085 overnight, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0840 GMT - The euro consolidates after last week's rally against the dollar as markets await news on German fiscal policy and Ukraine peace talks, ING analyst Chris Turner says in a note. A key area of focus is on whether German Chancellor-in-waiting Friedrich Merz can get his 500 billion-euro infrastructure fund through parliament, he says. He is in discussions with the Greens political party and the Bundestag lower house is expected to vote on the measures on March 18. "Headlines on whether the Greens are playing ball this week could trigger some volatility in the euro." The euro falls 0.2% to $1.0820 after hitting a four-month high of $1.0890 Friday, according to FactSet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0840 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds fall as investors expect more interest rate reductions from the Bank of England due to dwindling spending and growth. "Weak business and consumer sentiment may result in further rate cuts from the Bank of England," Lindsay James, investment strategist at Quilter Investors says in a note. The 10-year gilt yield falls 2 basis points to 4.637%, Tradeweb data shows. The 2-year gilt yield declines 1bp to 4.193%. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0815 GMT - India's consumer inflation is expected to have eased to 3.8% on year in February, according to the median estimate of 11 economists polled by The Wall Street Journal. That would compare with the 4.3% rise in January's CPI. Economists mostly expect broad-based declines in food and vegetable prices to drag inflation lower. Inflation is unlikely to rebound, giving the central bank room to ease rates to support the economy, Capital Economics' Shilan Shah and Joe Maher write in a note. "Weak domestic demand, higher crop output and muted manufacturing costs are likely to offset upside risks from a weak currency," Nomura research analysts say in a note. Both expect an additional 75 bps of rate cuts this year, which would bring the repo rate to 5.5% by the year's end. The inflation data are due Wednesday. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0814 GMT - There may be some hurdles for the Bank of Thailand to deliver a consecutive rate cut in April, Citi Research economists say in a note. The central bank had already factored in some of the risks associated with additional U.S. tariff hikes when delivering its surprise rate cut in February, they write. Citi lowers its 2025 and 2026 inflation forecasts to 0.9% and 1.0%, respectively, from 1.0% and 1.4%. Its 2025 inflation forecast is below the BOT's, reinforcing the risk of inflation undershooting the central bank's target for a second consecutive year. The pressure for another rate cut will persist, particularly as falling credit growth alleviates concerns about the financial stability costs of monetary easing, they say. Citi reiterates its expectation for another 25 bps cut in August.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0812 GMT - Eurozone 10-year government-bond yield spreads over German Bunds mostly narrow but should remain choppy amid European Union plans to ease debt rules, says Commerzbank Research's Rainer Guntermann in a note. "Eurozone government-bond spreads should also remain choppy as there could be more color about the EU's plan to relax debt rules from tonight's Eurogroup meeting," the rates strategist says. Germany is pushing for a permanent change of the EU's Stability and Growth Pact to exclude defense spending to avoid new joint debt, Guntermann says. The 10-year French OAT-German Bund yield spread falls 1.5 basis points to just below 72bps, while the 10-year Italian BTP-German Bund yield spread is flat at 111.5bps, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0806 GMT - The Canadian dollar could rise in the medium term if Canada's newly announced prime minister Mark Carney successfully counters U.S. tariffs, Swissquote Bank analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya says in a note. However, in the short-run the currency faces selling pressure as the Bank of Canada is expected to cut interest rates by another 25 basis points on Wednesday, she says. Carney was named prime minister after winning the Liberal Party leadership. The former governor of Bank of Canada and Bank of England succeeds Justin Trudeau, who announced in January that he would step down after his approval rating plummeted. Carney said he would keep retaliatory tariffs until "Americans show us respect." USD/CAD falls 0.1% to 1.4361. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0803 GMT - Germany's proposed increase in spending is likely to limit interest-rate cuts by the European Central Bank, ING analysts say in a note. The ECB is likely to keep interest rates on hold in April and make one cut before the summer, ING says. The planned additional spending is likely to boost economic growth, but it would take time for the positive effects to be seen, the analysts say. "Capital investment takes time to deploy; shovel-ready projects aren't always readily available." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0751 GMT - From a fair value perspective, ING rates strategists see strong reasons for the 10-year German Bund yield to rise towards 3%, with recent headlines and data points generally supporting higher yields, they say in a note. However, the tides could shift quickly, causing long-end government bond yields to fall faster than short-end bond yields--a phenomenon called "bull flattening," they say. "Any setbacks, be it in terms of actual spending, geopolitical tensions, or growth headwinds could easily bull flatten the curve again," the strategists say. The 10-year Bund yield last trades 2.2 bps higher at 2.853%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0750 GMT - The European Central Bank might be too pessimistic about the eurozone's growth chances this year as a planned spending drive looks set to boost activity, Pantheon Macroeconomics' Claus Vistesen and Melanie Debono write in a note to clients. The central bank last week cuts its estimates for output growth in the currency area, pointing notably to the risk of U.S. import tariffs. But those tariffs might not materialise in a sustained fashion, Vistesen and Debono says. And new plans in Germany and across the wider EU to spend more on infrastructure and defense offer a countervailing shot in the arm, they say. "[L]ast week's fiscal announcements point to more balanced risks further out," they say. (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: New Delhi Television Says Reliance Infrastructure Withdraws INR100 Billion Defamation Suit
data.description:
New Delhi Television said the defamation suit filed by Reliance Infrastructure and others, seeking 100 billion Indian rupees in damages over the company's coverage of the Rafale deal, has been unconditionally withdrawn.

The City Civil Court at Ahmedabad disposed of the case on March 8, according to an Indian bourse filing on Saturday.

data.title: European Truck Shares Fall Following Traton Guidance — Market Talk
data.description: Shares of European truck makers trade lower in early European trading after Traton says it expects the global economy will lose momentum this year. In Germany, shares of Daimler Truck are 3.2% down while in Italy, Iveco shares are around 2.1% lower. In Sweden, Volvo shares are down 2%. Traton says that after selling 1% fewer vehicles and achieving flat incoming orders last year, it expects demand for trucks to decline in its core markets this year. Traton shares trade 6.3% lower to 35.80 euros. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

data.title: Eneos Plans $3 Billion IPO of Metals Subsidiary — Update
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka

Eneos Holdings plans to spin off its metals subsidiary through an initial public offering that is expected to raise nearly $3 billion, the biggest listing in Japan in more than six years.

The Tokyo-based energy giant said Monday that it plans to offer a majority of shares in the wholly owned subsidiary, JX Advanced Metals, in the offering that would raise as much as 438.6 billion yen, equivalent to $2.96 billion. The listing is scheduled for March 19.

Eneos said in February that it would use part of the proceeds to fund investments needed to expand the supply of next-generation energy sources, such as synthetic fuels and hydrogen. No new shares will be issued.

The company has said that JX Advanced Metals, which makes materials used in the semiconductor and information-technology industries, will be able to accelerate capital investments as well as research and development as a listed company.

The Tokyo Stock Exchange hosted several large offerings in recent months after the benchmark Nikkei Stock Average hit record highs last year.

In October, the IPO by subway operator Tokyo Metro raised about $2.31 billion, making it the largest since SoftBank Corp.'s $21 billion IPO in 2018, according to data from Dealogic.

Eneos said it will sell up to 534.9 million shares of JX Advanced Metals, or 57.6% of the total outstanding, at Y820 each, equivalent to $5.54. JX Advanced Metals said earlier this month that it expected to offer shares at around Y815 apiece.

Also on Monday, Japan Post Holdings said it will offer $4 billion of its shares in its banking unit. The postal and financial services giant said a total of about 410 million shares of Japan Post Bank will be offered at Y1,444 each, valuing the offering at about Y592 billion, equivalent to $4 billion.

Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com

VietNam Holding Limited ("VNH" or the "Company")

The Company announces that, at its close of business on 10 Mar 2025, its Estimated NAV was USD 124.5 million or USD 5.277 per share and GBP 96.7 million or GBP 4.096 per share (GBP/USD = 1.2884).

Daily performance is calculated by reference to the previous day NAV/share and VNAS (Vietnam All Share Index). Calendar month-to-date performance is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 28 Feb 2025. Calendar year-to-date performance is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS at the previous calendar year end (31 Dec 2024). 1-year performance is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 10 Mar 2024. 3-year CAGR (compound annual growth rate) is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 10 Mar 2022. 5-year CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 10 Mar 2020. 10-year CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 10 Mar 2015. 15-year CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share and VNAS as of 10 Mar 2010. Since inception CAGR is calculated by reference to NAV/share as of 30 Jun 2006. VNAS is sourced from Bloomberg, which uses back-tested positions for VNAS for before 27 Jan 2014 as released by HoSE (Ho Chi Minh City Stock Exchange).

The Estimated Daily NAV stated in this announcement is based on estimated valuations and accordingly the Company accepts no responsibility for the accuracy of the Estimated Daily NAV figure given, and neither is any responsibility implied. The actual month end NAV in any month may vary from the Estimated Daily NAV provided in the relevant month. Accordingly, no reliance should be placed on the Estimated Daily NAV, and it has been provided for indicative purposes only.

More information on the Company is available at www.vietnamholding.com

data.title: Ford to inject up to $4.76 billion into German business, FT reports
data.description:
Ford Motor will inject up to EUR 4.4 billion ($4.76 billion) into its struggling German unit as it tries to revive its car business in Europe, the Financial Times reported on Monday.

($1 = 0.9249 euros)

data.title: Savannah Energy Closes Purchase of SIPEC in Nigeria
data.description:
Savannah Energy finalized its purchase of Sinopec International Petroleum Exploration and Production Co. Nigeria.

The $35.1 million deal will boost the lithium development company's reserves and resources by 30%, according to a Monday release. The acquired company's main asset is a 49% non-operated interest in the Stubb Creek oil & gas field in Akwa Ibom State, Nigeria.

The agreement was disclosed in March 2024.

data.description: Amazon.com has an average rating of buy and mean price target of $267.93, according to analysts polled by FactSet.

data.title: Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology Recorded Positive Net Profit for Single Quarter One Year Ahead of Target>9863.HK
Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology Achieved Positive Net Profit of CNY80M in 4Q24>9863.HK
Italian Stocks Edge Lower
data.description:
The FTSE MIB edged 0.2% lower to around 38,500 in early trading on Monday, as mounting concerns of a broader trade war and its impact on the U.S. economy kept investors on edge.

Additionally, China’s deflationary pressures further weighed on market sentiment.

Financial stocks led the decline, with UniCredit (-1.4%), Intesa Sanpaolo (-1.2%), FinecoBank (-2.6%), BPER Banca (-2.8%), Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (-2.2%), and Banca Generali (-1.7%) trading in the red.

On the other hand, notable gains were recorded by Leonardo (+1.7%), Amplifon (+1.5%), Hera (+2%), and Enel (+1.1%).

data.title: Italy's Monthly Industrial Producer Prices Increase in January
data.description:
Italy's industrial producer prices rose 1.6% month over month in January, following a 0.6% gain in the previous month, data from statistical agency Istat showed Monday.

On a yearly basis, the index was up 4.4%, compared with the prior 1.1% increase.

data.title: Zhejiang Leapmotor Technology Did Not Declare Final Dividend in 2024>9863.HK
News Highlights: Top Global Markets News of the Day - Monday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
U.S. stock futures fall after a rough week on Wall Street, as Trump won't rule out recession

U.S. stock futures fell Sunday, as President Donald Trump offered little reassurance after the S&P 500 had its worst week since September.

German Industrial Production Rose at Start of 2025 as Tariff Threats Loom

Output increased 2% on month in January as German industry showed tentative signs of improvement, though that could be threatened by U.S. trade tariffs.

Trump Declines to Rule Out Recession

In an interview that aired Sunday, the president demurred when asked if there were reassurances he could provide to businesses seeking more clarity on tariffs.

Tariff Wars Are Often Short. Their Legacies Aren't.

Economists fear that Trump's tariffs, once they take hold, could have unexpected effects long after he leaves office.

China Struggles to Shake Off Disinflationary Pressure as Trade Threats Loom

Consumer prices nosedived in February, ending a year of tepid price growth.

America's job market is eerily similar to the 1990s dot-com bubble - and, yes, it's a worry

March 20 marks the 25th anniversary of the dot-com bust. Could it happen again?

U.S. Bolsters Position as World's Top Arms Exporter

America has benefited from rising European spending, though President Trump's move to curb support for Ukraine is raising concerns about future purchases.

Stock Investors Go on Defense With Dividends

Some investors are sheltering from market swings in shares of companies with high payouts.

A Government Shutdown Could Create Short-Term Volatility. But It's Mostly Noise.

Tariffs aren't the only concern in Washington DC. A looming government shutdown could temporarily roil the markets too.

Tariffs Inflict Pain on Home Builders When They Can Least Afford It

Trade levies could add an extra $10,000 to the cost of building a home, likely coming out of builders' margins.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Monday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
Foxconn Builds FoxBrain, Its Own AI Model

The world's largest contract electronics maker said that aided by Nvidia it had built a large language model with reasoning capabilities, developed in-house and trained in four weeks.

Is the chip sector having a meltdown? By this measure, it's mostly just Nvidia.

Broadcom and Marvell shares have been hit recently. But Nvidia alone is responsible for about three quarters of the chip sector's lost market cap in the weeks since the DeepSeek news.

AI Profits Are Still a Mystery. An IPO Filing Has Clues.

A new IPO filing from start-up CoreWeave suggests that every dollar put into the AI cloud is quickly paying off thanks to rapid growth rates in the business.

7-Eleven Owner, Couche-Tard Financial Advisers Looking for Potential Buyers for Some Stores

Seven & i Holdings said in a letter to shareholders Monday that Couche-Tard recently agreed to jointly explore a potential sale of some stores prior to signing a takeover agreement.

Bally's Offers Financing Deal to Australia's Star

American betting and entertainment company Bally's has offered to provide embattled casino operator Star Entertainment with additional financing.

Tough to Digest? McDonald's and Yum! Brands Are at Record Highs Despite Consumer Concerns.

The two fast food stocks have surged this year. But can McDonald's and Yum keep satisfying investors' appetite for growth?

CVS's New Mini Stores Go All In on Medicine and Skip Everything Else

The national drugstore chain is preparing to open a dozen stores offering full-service pharmacies but very limited retail.

An Intel deal now seems less likely. These clues may hold the key to its future.

Investors appear to be now be discounting recent M&A speculation that Intel will split in two.

Robinhood to Pay $30 Million for Regulatory Violations, Finra Says

The regulator said Robinhood had violated "numerous" rules, including failing to respond to red flags for potential misconduct.

Activist Mantle Ridge Builds Over $1 Billion Stake in Tech-Services Firm Cognizant

The investor has been working behind the scenes at the tech-services firm and isn't currently planning to nominate directors.

data.title: Global Markets Start Week in Calm Mood Despite Doubts Over Strength of U.S. Economy
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff

A relatively calm start to the week across most assets, with U.S. inflation data due Wednesday and the University of Michigan's preliminary consumer confidence survey on Friday, as doubts grow about the strength of the U.S. economy.

President Trump, in an interview with Fox News on Sunday, refused to rule out a recession for the U.S. this year. The U.S. is set to impose steel and aluminum tariffs of 25% on the European Union from Wednesday.

  • Ahead of that, U.S. stock futures started the week lower, with Dow Jones Industrial Average futures off 0.6% and S&P 500 futures down 0.8%. Stocks in Europe opened either side of flat. Asian stock markets, apart from China, closed higher to start the week as worries over borrowing costs took a breather on comments by Fed chief Jerome Powell that the central bank will hold rates steady until there's greater economic certainty.
  • Data showing a drop in China's consumer prices weighed in on sentiment there; the Hong Kong's Hang Seng Index fell 1.8% and the Shanghai Composite Index slipped 0.2%.
  • The dollar steadied but remains at weaker levels after losses last week with the DXY dollar index suffering its worst week in two-and-a-half years; it was flat early Monday in Europe after hitting a four-month low of 103.458 Friday.
  • The Canadian dollar started the week broadly steady against the U.S. dollar amid prospects that Canada's next Prime Minister, Mark Carney, could successfully counter U.S. tariffs. Carney said he would keep retaliatory tariffs until "Americans show us respect."
  • The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield last traded at 4.297%, down three basis points, while the 10-year Bund yield was 2.2 basis points higher at 2.853%. The 10-year JGB yield rose 5.5 basis points to 1.575%, the highest level since October 2008 amid prospects of more Bank of Japan rate increases following Japanese wage data released earlier.
  • Bitcoin fell 5.5% to $81,605, according to LSEG. Widespread risk aversion is weighing on cryptocurrencies along with uncertainty over U.S. regulation following a crypto summit at the White House Friday.
  • Oil prices slipped, with Brent crude down 0.1% at $70.29 a barrel and WTI down 0.2% at $66.91 a barrel. Both benchmarks are down more than 7% on month.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Crude Stable After Recent Declines Amid Concerns Over Tariffs, OPEC+ Output Hike
data.description:
Crude oil prices held relatively steady early on Monday after a long downward trend amid concerns U.S. tariffs will hit global economic growth and fuel demand.

Brent crude edged 0.2% higher to US$70.50/barrel and West Texas Intermediate crude rose 0.3% to US$67.21/b at last look. Last week, WTI declined for a seventh straight week, the longest losing streak since November 2023, Reuters said in a Monday report.

Prices are also under pressure on speculation the U.S. will lift sanctions on Russia and the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries and allied producers will move forward with plans to boost production, the report said, citing IG analyst Tony Sycamore.

Meanwhile, Saudi Arabia lowered prices for crude grades sold to Asia for the first time in three months in April, in line with market expectations.

data.title: Market Outlook for the Week 10th - 14th March
data.description:
The week starts slowly with fewer scheduled economic events on Monday. Additionally, keep in mind the daylight saving time shift in the U.S. and Canada.

On Tuesday, Australia will release the Westpac consumer sentiment, Japan will report household spending y/y, and the U.S. will publish JOLTS job openings.

On Wednesday, the highlight will be the U.S. CPI data, while in Canada, the focus will be on the BoC monetary policy announcement. On Thursday, key U.S. data releases include the PPI m/m and unemployment claims.

Finally, on Friday, the U.K. will release GDP m/m, while in the U.S., the preliminary University of Michigan consumer sentiment and inflation expectations reports will be published.

In the U.S., the consensus for JOLTS job openings is 7.71M compared to the previous 7.60M.

In the last part of 2024 labor demand showed signs of stabilization despite a 500K drop in job openings in December, as job openings increased in Q4 overall.

Analysts at Wells Fargo note that job turnover remains historically low, as the post-pandemic job-switching trend has faded away. That said hiring and quit rates have flattened rather than declining further in recent months, indicating a possible inflection point.

No major decline is expected in this week's data, but economic policy uncertainty could put pressure on hiring and job-seeker activity, as firms and workers assess the impact of recent policy shifts before making decisions.

In the U.S., the consensus for core CPI m/m is 0.3% compared to the previous 0.4%. The CPI m/m is expected at 0.3% vs. the prior 0.5%, while CPI y/y is projected at 2.9%, slightly lower than the previous 3.0%.

U.S. inflation ran hot in January, though not as strong as during the same period last year. A few categories registered price surges, including prescription drugs, used cars, motor vehicle insurance and recreation services. However, some of these have subsequently eased in February.

One of the main drivers for February CPI were food prices, with weekly USDA data indicating no relief in egg prices. However, energy costs, especially gasoline rose less than their seasonal average and helped curb inflationary pressures, according to Wells Fargo.

While some core goods prices are expected to ease, early inflationary effects from new tariffs on Chinese imports implemented at the start of February could offset these declines. Concerns over tariffs are already influencing pricing decisions, keeping consumer price inflation on a firm trajectory.

In terms of monetary policy, the Fed is still expected to deliver another rate cut in June, but for now inflation remains above its desired target.

At this week's meeting, the BoC is expected to deliver a 25 bps rate cut, but there's also a strong possibility that it will keep rates on hold.

Analysts from Scotiabank emphasize that, given the rising risks to the Canadian economy from U.S.-imposed tariffs, the BoC is likely to strike a cautiously dovish tone and could delay future rate cuts until the full scope of the tariffs impact becomes clearer.

On the political front, former BoC and BoE governor Mark Carney won the Liberal Party's leadership race and will replace Justin Trudeau as Canada's prime minister. There is a high possibility that Carney will call for early federal elections.

Arguments in favor of a rate cut pause include signs of early recovery in domestic demand in 2025, supported by stronger-than-expected Q4 GDP growth. While employment data indicates some weakness in trade-sensitive sectors, the unemployment rate remains below late-2024 levels, suggesting a narrowing output gap—a key driver of future inflation in the BoC’s policy framework. Inflation remains close to the 2% target, though this has been partially influenced by the federal sales tax holiday and would otherwise be higher.

The recently announced 25% U.S. tariffs on Canada and Mexico are already being rolled back, with USMCA-compliant trade exempted. While only 38% of Canadian exports utilized USMCA last year, this will likely increase and it should be possible to reach over 90% relatively quickly, RBC estimates. However, new U.S. tariffs on steel and aluminum are expected over the coming week, with broader reciprocal tariffs likely in April. The ongoing uncertainty surrounding U.S. trade policy remains high, posing challenges for Canadian business investment.

RBC analysts argue that the BoC must balance the need to support the economy with the risk of adding to inflationary pressures. With the Overnight Rate at 3%, near the top of the neutral range (2.25%-3.25%), further rate cuts could stimulate the economy. However, Governor Macklem has emphasized that monetary policy alone cannot counteract tariff shocks, and fiscal policy may provide more targeted support.

In conclusion, the BoC’s decision remains uncertain. Trade risks could weigh on the economic outlook, but strong fundamentals may justify keeping rates steady for now.

In the U.S., the consensus for the preliminary UoM consumer sentiment is 63.8, down from the previous 64.7. The prior reading for the preliminary UoM inflation expectations was 4.3%. These surveys are being closely watched by Fed policymakers as softening consumer sentiment fuels concerns about economic growth.
